title: Token
description: The smallest unit of text a language model reads and predicts—usually a word piece, not always a whole word.
problemStatement: Models do not read raw characters or whole documents directly; they need a fixed vocabulary of units to embed and score.
coreIdea: A tokenizer maps text to integer token IDs; the model embeds those IDs and predicts the next token at each step.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A token is one step in the model's discrete text vocabulary. Subword tokenizers often split rare words into pieces so the vocabulary stays finite while still covering open vocabulary text.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Tokenization sets context length in tokens, shapes cost per request, and determines whether two strings look identical to the model. Mismatched tokenizers between training and serving are a common integration bug.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: The phrase "language models" might become two or three tokens depending on the tokenizer. Each ID is looked up in an embedding table before attention layers run.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Tokens appear at the input embedding layer, in autoregressive next-token prediction, and in KV-cache accounting during decoding. Attention modules operate on token positions, not raw characters.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Tokens are not always words; byte-level BPE can emit single-character tokens. Token count also differs from word count and from Unicode grapheme count.

readerShortcut.title: Reader Shortcut
readerShortcut.body: When someone says "128k context," they mean 128k tokens—the same units the tokenizer emits and the model scores.

conceptMap.alt: Diagram showing text flowing through a tokenizer into token IDs and embeddings
conceptMap.caption: How raw text becomes token IDs before the transformer stack
